speechOptions.instruction: Speak in a bright, warm, hopeful tone — like happily introducing something you love to a friend. Gentle, positive energy throughout; never dark or flat.

title: Tomorrow's Weather Is Already in Your Plans
description: Real examples of MulmoClaude's weather × plans: the umbrella nudge, skipped watering, a weekend green light, and an Osaka trip rescheduled ahead of a typhoon — three minutes of life connected to the weather.

beats.text: In MulmoClaude, the weather forecast isn't something you just look at. It connects to your plans and your to-dos, woven in before you wake. Here is the story of a life connected to the weather, in three minutes.

beats.text: You check the forecast every morning. But holding it up against your plans — the umbrella calls, the logistics — was always your job. In MulmoClaude, the forecast and your plans live inside the same AI. So when the weather changes, the news reaches your plans.

beats.text: Take a Monday morning. "You have an outing at 3 p.m. today. Rain starts this afternoon, so don't forget your umbrella" — that's the note. It isn't a plain weather alert — it reads your plans, and it arrives only on mornings when the umbrella matters.

beats.text: Wednesday brings rain from the morning. Look at the to-do list, and the garden watering is gone. That's because the list is connected to the forecast. In a heat-wave week, it goes the other way — watering doubles to morning and evening. Removing things, adding things: both can be left to it.

MulmoClaude · Life, connected to the weather

beats.text: On Friday night, the weekend outlook arrives. "Sunday's picnic looks sunny. You're good to go as planned. I've added sunscreen to the packing list". No more digging through forecasts wondering whether it's on.

beats.text: The next Monday, a typhoon formed over the southern sea. "Thursday's Osaka trip may end up inside the typhoon's reach. The track isn't settled yet, though. Let's look again at tonight's forecast update". No panic — but someone is clearly keeping watch.

MulmoClaude · Life, connected to the weather

beats.text: That night, the track was settled. Thursday would put the Kansai region inside the storm zone all day. Flights would be grounded, and even the bullet train might stop. MulmoClaude's proposal: move the whole trip up a day, to Wednesday. Take the bullet train back and be in Tokyo by evening. It even prepared a draft of the email asking your counterpart to reschedule.

unsent
To: your meeting counterpart
Subject: Moving Thursday's visit up to Wednesday
With a typhoon forecast to approach, might we move Thursday's meeting up to Wednesday, the day before? Would that work on your end? —
✍Only a draft — whether to send it is your call
* Mock-up — to be replaced with a real screen capture
MulmoClaude · Life, connected to the weather

beats.text: Next morning, the reply came back: "Wednesday works fine". You pick from the bullet-train options and book it yourself. Tell it "got the ticket", and the calendar and the trip notes rewrite themselves around the new plan.

beats.text: Wednesday evening, meeting done, you boarded the bullet train home. The next morning you woke to a storm lashing the windows. The trip was already finished, a day ago. Not because the weather pushed your plans around — because the weather was woven into them.

beats.text: There was no special setup. The weather forecast and your plans are simply collections inside the same AI. So when the weather changes, the plans are what move. Even at night, while you're asleep.

MulmoClaude · Life, connected to the weather

beats.text: From mornings spent weighing your plans against the sky. To mornings that begin with the weather already folded in. To start, just tell MulmoClaude: "connect the weather to my plans".
